---
title: "Indexing & selection"
module: "M3 — Data & Tooling"
chapter: 3
difficulty: "beginner"
prereqs:
  - "M3 Ch2 — Pandas Series & DataFrame"
learning_objectives:
  - "Select columns and rows out of a DataFrame"
  - "Use .loc to select by label and .iloc to select by position"
  - "Filter rows with a boolean mask"
compute: "browser"
status: "stable"
last_reviewed: "2026-06-29"
est_minutes: 16
resources:
  - ../../lib/grader.py
  - ../../lib/data.py
  - data/penguins.csv
pyodide:
  packages:
    - pandas
review_cards:
  - id: loc-vs-iloc
    q: "What is the difference between .loc and .iloc?"
    a: ".loc selects by label (the index value or column name) and its slices are inclusive of the end; .iloc selects by integer position (0-based) and its slices stop before the end, like ordinary Python."
  - id: boolean-mask
    q: "What does a boolean mask do when you pass it to df[...]?"
    a: "The mask is a Series of True/False, one per row. df[mask] keeps only the rows where the mask is True and drops the rest, so it filters the table down to the rows you care about."
  - id: select-a-column
    q: "How do you select a single column from a DataFrame, and what comes back?"
    a: "Index the DataFrame with the column name in square brackets, df['col']. What comes back is a Series — that one labelled column, with the index attached."
format:
  live-html:
    theme:
      light:
        - cosmo
        - ../../theme/larnix.scss
      dark:
        - darkly
        - ../../theme/larnix-dark.scss
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    code-copy: true
    code-overflow: wrap
    include-after-body: ../../theme/_after-body.html
execute:
  enabled: false
---

{{< badge difficulty=beginner >}} {{< badge compute=browser >}} {{< badge status=stable >}}

## The hook

A table is rarely interesting all at once. The questions you actually ask are
small slices of it: *what species is in row 0?* *show me the first five rows.*
*which penguins weigh more than four kilograms?* Each of those is a **selection** —
you point at part of the table and pandas hands you back exactly that part.

In the last chapter you loaded the Palmer Penguins table and sized it up. Now you
learn to *reach into* it. There are three moves, and almost everything you do with
data is a combination of them:

- **Pick a column** by name — `df['species']`.
- **Pick rows** — by their **position** with `.iloc`, or by their **label** with
  `.loc`.
- **Filter rows** with a **boolean mask** — keep only the rows that pass a test,
  like `body_mass_g > 4000`.

Get these three reflexes and a DataFrame stops being a wall of numbers and becomes
something you can interrogate.

> You'll need from before: a DataFrame is a table of named columns, and `df['col']`
> returns one column as a Series (M3 Ch2). We build straight on that.

---

## Picking a column

The simplest selection is one column, by name, in square brackets. What comes back
is a **Series** — the labelled column you met last chapter.

```{pyodide}
#| caption: "Select one column by name — the result is a Series"
from lib.data import load_csv
import pandas as pd

df = load_csv("penguins")
species = df["species"]
print(type(species).__name__)
species.head()
```

`df["species"]` is a Series, so every Series tool carries over. A column of text is
often best summarised by counting its values:

```{pyodide}
#| caption: "A column is a Series, so its summaries apply directly"
from lib.data import load_csv
import pandas as pd

df = load_csv("penguins")
df["species"].value_counts()
```

That tallies **Adelie 152, Gentoo 124, Chinstrap 68**. To pull *several* columns at
once, pass a **list** of names inside the brackets — note the double brackets, an
outer pair for indexing and an inner pair for the list. The result is a smaller
DataFrame, not a Series:

```{pyodide}
#| caption: "A list of names selects several columns — the result is a DataFrame"
from lib.data import load_csv
import pandas as pd

df = load_csv("penguins")
df[["species", "body_mass_g"]].head()
```

One name in brackets gives a Series; a list of names gives a DataFrame. Keep that
distinction in mind — it explains a lot of "why did I get a different shape?"
surprises later.

## Picking rows by position with `.iloc`

Columns have names, but rows are easiest to reach by **position**: the first row,
the first five rows, the last one. That is what `.iloc` does — **i** for *integer
position*. It works like indexing a Python list, and it is 0-based, so the
first row is position `0`:

```{pyodide}
#| caption: ".iloc[0] — the first row, returned as a Series"
from lib.data import load_csv
import pandas as pd

df = load_csv("penguins")
df.iloc[0]
```

A single row comes back as a Series, with the **column names** now acting as its
index. Reading down it, row 0 is an **Adelie** penguin seen on Torgersen Island.

To grab a *range* of rows, slice with a colon. `.iloc[:5]` means "positions 0 up to
but not including 5" — the same half-open slicing Python lists use, so it returns
exactly five rows:

```{pyodide}
#| caption: ".iloc[:5] — the first five rows, like list slicing"
from lib.data import load_csv
import pandas as pd

df = load_csv("penguins")
df.iloc[:5]
```

This is exactly what `df.head()` gave you last chapter; `.iloc` makes the
positions explicit, so you can also ask for `df.iloc[10:13]` or the last row with
`df.iloc[-1]`. Position-based selection never cares what the row labels are — only
where the rows sit.

## Picking rows by label with `.loc`

`.loc` selects by **label** — the value in the row index, and the column name. For
the penguins table the index is the default `0, 1, 2, …`, so a label often *looks*
like a position, but it is a different idea: `.loc` asks "the row labelled `0`",
not "the row in position 0". You can name the column in the same call:

```{pyodide}
#| caption: ".loc[label, 'column'] — select by row label and column name"
from lib.data import load_csv
import pandas as pd

df = load_csv("penguins")
print(df.loc[0, "species"])
df.loc[0:4, ["species", "body_mass_g"]]
```

Two things to notice. First, `df.loc[0, "species"]` reaches a single cell — the
species of the row labelled `0`, which is `Adelie`. Second, `df.loc[0:4, ...]`
returns **five** rows, labels `0` through `4` — `.loc` slices are **inclusive** of
the end label. That is the one gotcha worth memorising:

- `.iloc[0:4]` → positions 0, 1, 2, 3 — **four** rows (end excluded).
- `.loc[0:4]` → labels 0, 1, 2, 3, 4 — **five** rows (end included).

Use `.iloc` when you think in *positions* ("the first ten rows") and `.loc` when you
think in *labels* ("rows 0 through 4, and only these two columns"). Mixing them up
is the most common early pandas bug, and naming which one you mean fixes it.

## Filtering rows with a boolean mask

The selection you'll reach for most is the **filter**: keep only the rows that meet
a condition. It happens in two steps. First, compare a column to a value — this runs
the test on every row at once and produces a **boolean mask**, a Series of
`True`/`False`:

```{pyodide}
#| caption: "Comparing a column to a value builds a boolean mask"
from lib.data import load_csv
import pandas as pd

df = load_csv("penguins")
mask = df["body_mass_g"] > 4000
mask.head()
```

The mask has one entry per row: `True` where the penguin weighs more than 4000 g,
`False` otherwise. Now pass that mask back into the DataFrame in square brackets, and
pandas keeps only the `True` rows:

```{pyodide}
#| caption: "df[mask] keeps the rows where the mask is True"
from lib.data import load_csv
import pandas as pd

df = load_csv("penguins")
heavy = df[df["body_mass_g"] > 4000]
print("rows kept:", heavy.shape[0])
heavy.head()
```

Out of 344 penguins, **172** weigh more than 4000 g. You can read
`df[df["body_mass_g"] > 4000]` as a single sentence: "from `df`, take the rows where
`body_mass_g` is over 4000." Filtering with `==` works the same way for text — this
is how you isolate one species:

```{pyodide}
#| caption: "Filter by an exact match, then read a column off the subset"
from lib.data import load_csv
import pandas as pd

df = load_csv("penguins")
gentoo = df[df["species"] == "Gentoo"]
print("Gentoo penguins:", gentoo.shape[0])
print("their mean body mass:", round(gentoo["body_mass_g"].mean(), 2))
```

That filter keeps **124** Gentoo penguins, and selecting the `body_mass_g` column of
that subset and averaging it gives a mean of about **5076.02** g — Gentoos are
the heavyweights of this dataset, well above the 4201.75 g average across all three
species. Combining a mask (to choose rows) with a column selection (to choose what to
measure) is the core loop of exploratory data analysis: *narrow the rows, then read a
number off them.*

---

## Key Takeaways

::: {.key-takeaways}
### Key Takeaways

1. `df['col']` selects one column as a **Series**; `df[['a', 'b']]` (a list of
   names) selects several columns as a **DataFrame**.
2. **`.iloc`** selects by integer **position** (0-based, end-exclusive slices, like
   Python lists); **`.loc`** selects by **label** (and its slices are
   **inclusive** of the end label).
3. A **boolean mask** — `df['col'] > value` — is a Series of True/False; `df[mask]`
   keeps only the rows that are True.
4. Narrow the rows with a mask, then read a column off the subset — for penguins,
   `df[df['species'] == 'Gentoo']` keeps **124** rows.
:::

---

## Practice

Each exercise is auto-graded by `run_tests`, loaded for you from `lib/grader.py`.
The first two use tiny hand-made data so the answers are exact.

### Exercise 1 — Guided (≈4 min) 🟢

Build a DataFrame from a dictionary, take its first `n` rows with `.head(n)`, and
return how many rows that is as an `int`. Replace `____`, then press **Run**.

```{pyodide}
#| setup: true
#| exercise: ex_first_n
from lib.grader import run_tests
```

```{pyodide}
#| exercise: ex_first_n
import pandas as pd

def first_n(data, n):
    """Build a DataFrame from data and return the row count of its first n rows."""
    return ____  # TODO: int(pd.DataFrame(data).head(n).shape[0])

run_tests([
    ("first 2", first_n({"a": [1, 2, 3, 4]}, 2), 2),
    ("first 0", first_n({"a": [1, 2, 3, 4]}, 0), 0),
])
```

<details><summary>If you're stuck</summary>

Build the table with `pd.DataFrame(data)`, take the first rows with `.head(n)`, then
read the row count from `.shape[0]` (the first item of the `(rows, columns)` tuple).
Wrap it in `int(...)` so the return type is a plain integer.
</details>

<details><summary>Show solution</summary>

```python
import pandas as pd

def first_n(data, n):
    return int(pd.DataFrame(data).head(n).shape[0])
```

`pd.DataFrame(data)` builds the table, `.head(n)` keeps the first `n` rows, and
`.shape[0]` is the number of rows that remain. Asking for `head(0)` keeps no rows, so
the count is `0`.
</details>

### Exercise 2 — Implement (≈8 min) 🟡

Write `count_above(data, col, threshold)`: build a DataFrame, then return how many
rows have `col` **greater than** `threshold`. This is the boolean-mask pattern —
compare a column, then count the `True` values. Complete the marked line.

```{pyodide}
#| setup: true
#| exercise: ex_count_above
from lib.grader import run_tests
```

```{pyodide}
#| exercise: ex_count_above
import pandas as pd

def count_above(data, col, threshold):
    """Return the number of rows where data[col] exceeds threshold."""
    return ____  # TODO: int((pd.DataFrame(data)[col] > threshold).sum())

run_tests([
    ("two above 2", count_above({"x": [1, 2, 3, 4]}, "x", 2), 2),
    ("none above 9", count_above({"x": [1, 2, 3, 4]}, "x", 9), 0),
])
```

<details><summary>If you're stuck</summary>

`pd.DataFrame(data)[col]` is the column as a Series. Comparing it with `> threshold`
gives a boolean mask (True/False per row). Calling `.sum()` on a boolean Series
counts the `True` values, because `True` counts as 1; wrap that in `int(...)`.
</details>

<details><summary>Show solution</summary>

```python
import pandas as pd

def count_above(data, col, threshold):
    return int((pd.DataFrame(data)[col] > threshold).sum())
```

`(pd.DataFrame(data)[col] > threshold)` is the boolean mask; `.sum()` adds up its
`True` values (each worth 1). With threshold 2 the values 3 and 4 pass, so the count
is `2`; with threshold 9 none pass, so it is `0`.
</details>

### Exercise 3 — Stretch (optional, ≈8 min) 🔴

Open-ended, **rubric-graded** (not auto-checked). Load the penguins dataset, filter
it down to the **Gentoo** penguins with a boolean mask, and report two facts in your
own `print` statements: **how many** Gentoo penguins there are (124) and their
**mean body mass** (about 5076.02 g).

**Rubric — a good answer:**

- Loads penguins with `load_csv` and builds a mask `df["species"] == "Gentoo"`.
- Filters with `df[mask]` and prints the row count (124) read from the data.
- Selects `body_mass_g` on that subset and prints its mean (≈ 5076.02 g).

<details><summary>If you're stuck</summary>

Build the mask `df["species"] == "Gentoo"`, then `df[mask]` is the Gentoo-only table.
Its `.shape[0]` is the count; selecting `["body_mass_g"]` on that subset and calling
`.mean()` gives the average mass.
</details>

<details><summary>Show one possible answer</summary>

```python
from lib.data import load_csv
import pandas as pd

df = load_csv("penguins")
gentoo = df[df["species"] == "Gentoo"]

print("Gentoo penguins:", gentoo.shape[0])
print("mean body mass (g):", round(gentoo["body_mass_g"].mean(), 2))
```

This prints `124` Gentoo penguins and a mean body mass of `5076.02` g — both read
straight from the filtered subset rather than typed in by hand.
</details>

---

## Check yourself

{{< quiz quiz-ch03.yml >}}

---

## Where this fits / what's next

You can now reach into a table three ways: pick columns by name, pick rows by
position (`.iloc`) or label (`.loc`), and filter rows with a boolean mask. Those moves
combine into nearly every question you'll ask of data. Next we go from *selecting*
what's already there to *cleaning* it — handling the missing values that `.describe()`
quietly skipped, so your selections and summaries rest on solid ground.
